packs.name: PostgreSQL
packs.description: Protects against destructive PostgreSQL operations like DROP DATABASE, TRUNCATE, and dropdb

destructive_patterns.name: drop-database
destructive_patterns.reason: DROP DATABASE permanently deletes the entire database (even with IF EXISTS). Verify and back up first.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
DROP DATABASE completely removes a database and ALL its contents:

- All tables, views, and indexes
- All functions, procedures, and triggers
- All data - gone permanently
- Users/roles remain but lose access

IF EXISTS only prevents errors if the database doesn't exist - it still deletes!

Before dropping:
  pg_dump -h host -U user dbname > backup.sql

Verify database name:
  psql -c '\l' | grep dbname

suggestions.description: Create a full backup before dropping
Verify database name before dropping

suggestions.command: SELECT datname FROM pg_database WHERE datname = '{dbname}'
suggestions.description: Check if database exists

destructive_patterns.name: drop-table
destructive_patterns.reason: DROP TABLE permanently deletes the table (even with IF EXISTS). Verify and back up first.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
DROP TABLE removes the table structure and ALL data:

- All rows are deleted
- Indexes, constraints, triggers are removed
- Foreign keys referencing this table may fail
- CASCADE drops dependent objects too

IF EXISTS only prevents errors - it still drops the table!

Backup table first:
  pg_dump -t tablename dbname > table_backup.sql

Preview table contents:
  SELECT COUNT(*) FROM tablename;
  SELECT * FROM tablename LIMIT 10;

suggestions.description: Backup the table before dropping
Check row count before dropping
Review table structure (in psql)
Preview table contents

destructive_patterns.name: drop-schema
destructive_patterns.reason: DROP SCHEMA permanently deletes the schema and all its objects (even with IF EXISTS).
destructive_patterns.explanation:
DROP SCHEMA removes a schema and potentially ALL objects within it:

- With CASCADE: Drops all tables, views, functions in the schema
- With RESTRICT (default): Fails if schema is not empty
- public schema deletion is catastrophic

List schema contents first:
  SELECT table_name FROM information_schema.tables 
  WHERE table_schema = 'schema_name';

Backup schema:
  pg_dump -n schema_name dbname > schema_backup.sql

suggestions.description: Backup schema before dropping

suggestions.command: SELECT table_name FROM information_schema.tables WHERE table_schema = '{schema_name}'
suggestions.description: List all tables in the schema
Use RESTRICT to fail if schema is not empty

destructive_patterns.name: truncate-table
destructive_patterns.reason: TRUNCATE permanently deletes all rows without logging individual deletions.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
TRUNCATE is faster than DELETE but more dangerous:

- Removes ALL rows instantly
- Cannot be rolled back outside a transaction
- Does not fire DELETE triggers
- Resets IDENTITY/SERIAL columns
- CASCADE truncates referencing tables too

TRUNCATE is transactional in PostgreSQL. Wrap in transaction:
  BEGIN;
  TRUNCATE tablename;
  -- verify, then COMMIT or ROLLBACK

Check row count first:
  SELECT COUNT(*) FROM tablename;

suggestions.description: Check how many rows would be deleted
Wrap in transaction for rollback capability
Backup data before truncating

destructive_patterns.name: delete-without-where
destructive_patterns.reason: DELETE without WHERE clause deletes ALL rows. Add a WHERE clause or use TRUNCATE intentionally.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
DELETE without WHERE removes ALL rows from the table:

- Each row deletion is logged (slower than TRUNCATE)
- Can be rolled back within a transaction
- Fires DELETE triggers for each row
- Does not reset IDENTITY/SERIAL counters

If you meant to delete all rows, use TRUNCATE for speed.
Otherwise, add a WHERE clause:
  DELETE FROM tablename WHERE condition;

Preview what would be deleted:
  SELECT COUNT(*) FROM tablename;  -- all rows!
  SELECT * FROM tablename LIMIT 10;

suggestions.description: Add a WHERE clause to limit deletion
Check how many rows exist
Use TRUNCATE if you truly want all rows deleted (faster)

suggestions.command: BEGIN; DELETE FROM {tablename}; -- ROLLBACK or COMMIT
suggestions.description: Wrap in transaction for rollback capability

destructive_patterns.name: dropdb-cli
destructive_patterns.reason: dropdb permanently deletes the entire database. Verify the database name carefully.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
dropdb is the CLI equivalent of DROP DATABASE:

- Completely removes the database
- All data is lost permanently
- No confirmation prompt by default
- Cannot be undone

Triple-check the database name. Common mistake:
  dropdb myapp_production  # Oops, meant myapp_staging

Backup first:
  pg_dump -h host -U user dbname > backup.sql

List databases to verify:
  psql -c '\l'

suggestions.description: Create a full backup before dropping
List databases to verify the correct one
Check database size before dropping

destructive_patterns.name: pg-dump-clean
destructive_patterns.reason: pg_dump --clean drops objects before creating them. This can be destructive on restore.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
pg_dump --clean adds DROP statements to the backup file. On restore:

- DROP TABLE is run before CREATE TABLE
- Existing data is deleted before restore
- If restore fails partway, data may be lost

This is safe for backup, but dangerous when restoring to a database with existing data you want to keep.

Safer approach for restoring:
- Restore to a new database first
- Verify the restore
- Then swap databases

Without --clean:
  pg_dump dbname > backup.sql  # Creates only, no drops

suggestions.description: Create backup without DROP statements
Restore to a new database first, then verify

packs.name: MySQL/MariaDB
packs.description: Protects against destructive MySQL/MariaDB operations like DROP DATABASE, TRUNCATE, and mysqladmin drop

destructive_patterns.name: drop-database
destructive_patterns.reason: DROP DATABASE permanently deletes the entire database. Verify and back up first.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
DROP DATABASE completely removes a database and ALL its contents:

- All tables, views, and indexes
- All stored procedures and functions
- All triggers and events
- All data - gone permanently
- User privileges on the database remain but are orphaned

IF EXISTS only prevents errors if the database doesn't exist - it still deletes!

Before dropping:
  mysqldump -h host -u user -p dbname > backup.sql

Verify database name:
  SHOW DATABASES LIKE 'dbname';

suggestions.description: Create a full backup before dropping
Verify database name before dropping

suggestions.command: SELECT table_name FROM information_schema.tables WHERE table_schema = '{dbname}'
suggestions.description: List all tables in the database

destructive_patterns.name: drop-table
destructive_patterns.reason: DROP TABLE permanently deletes the table. Verify and back up first.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
DROP TABLE removes the table structure and ALL data:

- All rows are permanently deleted
- Indexes and constraints are removed
- Foreign key relationships may break
- Triggers on the table are dropped
- AUTO_INCREMENT values are lost

IF EXISTS only prevents errors - it still drops the table!

Backup table first:
  mysqldump -h host -u user -p dbname tablename > table_backup.sql

Preview table contents:
  SELECT COUNT(*) FROM tablename;
  SELECT * FROM tablename LIMIT 10;

suggestions.description: Backup the table before dropping
Check row count before dropping
Review table structure before dropping
Preview table contents

destructive_patterns.name: truncate-table
destructive_patterns.reason: TRUNCATE permanently deletes all rows. Cannot be rolled back in MySQL.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
TRUNCATE is faster than DELETE but more dangerous in MySQL:

- Removes ALL rows instantly
- CANNOT be rolled back (implicit commit)
- Does not fire DELETE triggers
- Resets AUTO_INCREMENT counter to 1
- Requires DROP privilege
- Acquires table lock

Unlike PostgreSQL, MySQL's TRUNCATE is NOT transactional!

Check row count first:
  SELECT COUNT(*) FROM tablename;

If you need rollback capability, use:
  DELETE FROM tablename;  -- Slower but transactional

suggestions.description: Check how many rows would be deleted
Use DELETE for transactional, recoverable deletion
Backup data to temporary table before truncating

destructive_patterns.name: delete-without-where
destructive_patterns.reason: DELETE without WHERE clause deletes ALL rows. Add a WHERE clause.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
DELETE without WHERE removes ALL rows from the table:

- Each row deletion is logged in binary log
- CAN be rolled back if within a transaction
- Fires DELETE triggers for each row
- Does NOT reset AUTO_INCREMENT counter
- Much slower than TRUNCATE for large tables

If you meant to delete all rows:
- TRUNCATE is faster (but non-transactional)
- DELETE in transaction allows rollback

Otherwise, add a WHERE clause:
  DELETE FROM tablename WHERE condition;

Preview what would be deleted:
  SELECT COUNT(*) FROM tablename;  -- all rows!
  SELECT * FROM tablename LIMIT 10;

suggestions.description: Add a WHERE clause to limit deletion
Check how many rows exist before deletion
Use TRUNCATE if you truly want to delete all rows (faster)
Preview table contents before deletion

destructive_patterns.name: mysqladmin-drop
destructive_patterns.reason: mysqladmin drop permanently deletes the database. Verify carefully.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
mysqladmin drop is the CLI equivalent of DROP DATABASE:

- Completely removes the database
- All data is lost permanently
- Has an 'are you sure?' prompt by default
- Adding --force skips confirmation!

Triple-check the database name. Common mistake:
  mysqladmin drop myapp_production  # Oops, meant myapp_staging

Backup first:
  mysqldump -h host -u user -p dbname > backup.sql

List databases to verify:
  mysql -e 'SHOW DATABASES;'

suggestions.description: Create a full backup before dropping
List databases to verify the correct one
Review tables in the database

destructive_patterns.name: mysqldump-add-drop-database
destructive_patterns.reason: mysqldump --add-drop-database drops the database before restore.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
mysqldump --add-drop-database adds DROP DATABASE to the backup file.

On restore, this will:
- DROP the database before CREATE DATABASE
- Destroy ALL existing data in that database
- If restore fails partway, data may be lost

This is dangerous when restoring to a database with data you want to keep.

Safer approach:
- Restore to a new/different database first
- Verify the restore completed successfully
- Then rename or swap databases

Without --add-drop-database:
  mysqldump dbname > backup.sql  # Creates only, no drops

suggestions.description: Create backup without DROP DATABASE statement
Restore to a new database first, then verify

destructive_patterns.name: mysqldump-add-drop-table
destructive_patterns.reason: mysqldump --add-drop-table drops tables before creating them on restore.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
mysqldump --add-drop-table adds DROP TABLE before each CREATE TABLE.

This is actually the DEFAULT behavior of mysqldump!
On restore, each table is dropped before being recreated.

Risks:
- If restore fails partway, some tables may be dropped without recreation
- Existing data in those tables is permanently lost

Safer alternatives:
- Use --skip-add-drop-table to disable drops
- Restore to a new database first, then verify
- Keep the original database until restore is confirmed

To preserve existing data:
  mysqldump --skip-add-drop-table dbname > backup.sql

suggestions.description: Create backup without DROP TABLE statements
Default dump includes DROP TABLE (use --skip-add-drop-table to disable)

destructive_patterns.name: grant-all
destructive_patterns.reason: GRANT ALL ON *.* gives unrestricted access to all databases.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
GRANT ALL PRIVILEGES ON *.* gives the user complete control:

- Can create, modify, and drop ANY database
- Can create, modify, and drop ANY user
- Can grant privileges to others
- Essentially creates a superuser

This is rarely necessary and violates principle of least privilege.

Better alternatives:
- GRANT ALL ON specific_db.* - Limit to one database
- GRANT SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE ON db.* - Limit operations
- GRANT ... ON db.table - Limit to specific tables

Review current grants:
  SHOW GRANTS FOR 'user'@'host';

suggestions.description: Limit privileges to a specific database

suggestions.command: GRANT SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE ON {db}.* TO '{user}'@'{host}'
suggestions.description: Grant only needed privileges
Review current grants before adding more

destructive_patterns.name: drop-user
destructive_patterns.reason: DROP USER permanently removes the user account and all their privileges.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
DROP USER removes the MySQL user account:

- All privileges granted to the user are revoked
- User can no longer authenticate
- Applications using this user will fail
- Cannot be undone (user must be recreated)

IF EXISTS only prevents errors if user doesn't exist.

Before dropping:
  SHOW GRANTS FOR 'user'@'host';  -- Document privileges
  SELECT * FROM mysql.user WHERE user='username';  -- Verify user

Consider disabling instead:
  ALTER USER 'user'@'host' ACCOUNT LOCK;

suggestions.description: Document privileges before dropping
Disable account instead of dropping
Verify user exists and check details

destructive_patterns.name: reset-master
destructive_patterns.reason: RESET MASTER deletes all binary logs and resets the binlog position.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
RESET MASTER is destructive to replication and point-in-time recovery:

- Deletes ALL binary log files
- Resets binary log index
- Clears GTID executed set
- Breaks replication to all replicas
- Loses point-in-time recovery capability

Replicas must be reconfigured after RESET MASTER on the source.

Before running:
- Ensure no replicas are replicating
- Backup binary logs if needed for recovery
- Consider PURGE BINARY LOGS for selective cleanup

Safer alternative:
  PURGE BINARY LOGS BEFORE 'date';  -- Keeps recent logs

suggestions.description: Selectively purge old logs instead of all
List current binary logs before reset
Check replica status before resetting master

packs.name: MongoDB
packs.description: Protects against destructive MongoDB operations like dropDatabase, dropCollection, and remove without criteria

destructive_patterns.name: drop-database
destructive_patterns.reason: dropDatabase permanently deletes the entire database.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
dropDatabase() removes the current database completely:

- All collections are dropped
- All indexes are removed
- All documents are gone
- Users scoped to the database lose access

This cannot be undone. The database can be recreated but data is lost.

Backup first:
  mongodump --db=dbname --out=/path/to/backup

List databases:
  show dbs

destructive_patterns.name: drop-collection
destructive_patterns.reason: drop/dropCollection permanently deletes the collection.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
drop() removes the entire collection:

- All documents are deleted
- All indexes are removed
- Schema validation rules are lost
- Cannot be undone

Backup collection first:
  mongodump --db=dbname --collection=collname

Count documents before dropping:
  db.collection.countDocuments()

destructive_patterns.name: delete-all
destructive_patterns.reason: remove({}) or deleteMany({}) deletes ALL documents. Add filter criteria.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
An empty filter {} matches ALL documents in the collection:

- deleteMany({}) removes every document
- remove({}) (deprecated) does the same
- Collection structure and indexes remain

If you want to delete everything, drop() is faster.
Otherwise, add filter criteria:
  db.collection.deleteMany({ status: 'expired' })

Preview what would be deleted:
  db.collection.countDocuments({})  // All documents!
  db.collection.find({}).limit(10)  // Sample docs

destructive_patterns.name: mongorestore-drop
destructive_patterns.reason: mongorestore --drop deletes existing data before restoring.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
mongorestore --drop drops collections before restoring them:

- Existing data is deleted first
- If restore fails partway, data may be lost
- Only affects collections being restored

Safer approaches:
- Restore to a different database first
- Use --nsExclude to skip certain collections
- Remove --drop and handle conflicts manually

Test restore:
  mongorestore --db=test_restore --drop /backup/path

destructive_patterns.name: collection-drop
destructive_patterns.reason: collection.drop() permanently deletes the collection.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
db.<collection>.drop() is the most common way to delete a collection:

- All documents in the collection are deleted
- Indexes on the collection are removed
- Cannot be undone

Before dropping:
  db.collection.stats()           // Size and document count
  db.collection.find().limit(5)   // Sample documents

Backup:
  mongodump --db=mydb --collection=mycollection

packs.name: Redis
packs.description: Protects against destructive Redis operations like FLUSHALL, FLUSHDB, and mass key deletion

destructive_patterns.name: flushall
destructive_patterns.reason: FLUSHALL permanently deletes ALL keys in ALL databases.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
FLUSHALL removes every key from every database (0-15 by default):

- All cached data is lost
- All session data is gone
- All queues are emptied
- Affects ALL databases, not just current

This is instant and cannot be undone. RDB/AOF backups are not affected until the next save.

Check database sizes first:
  INFO keyspace
  DBSIZE

Consider:
  FLUSHDB  # Only current database
  DEL key1 key2  # Specific keys

destructive_patterns.name: flushdb
destructive_patterns.reason: FLUSHDB permanently deletes ALL keys in the current database.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
FLUSHDB removes all keys from the currently selected database:

- All keys in this database are deleted
- Other databases (0-15) are unaffected
- Instant execution, no confirmation
- Cannot be undone

Check current database and key count:
  CLIENT INFO          # Shows current DB
  DBSIZE               # Keys in current DB
  KEYS '*' | head -20  # Sample keys (slow on large DBs)

Consider SCAN + DEL for selective deletion.

destructive_patterns.name: debug-crash
destructive_patterns.reason: DEBUG SEGFAULT/CRASH will crash the Redis server.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
DEBUG SEGFAULT/CRASH intentionally crashes the Redis server:

- Server process terminates immediately
- All connections are dropped
- Unsaved data may be lost
- Services depending on Redis fail

This is meant for testing crash recovery, not production use.

These commands should be disabled in production:
  CONFIG SET debug-command no
  rename-command DEBUG ""  # In redis.conf

destructive_patterns.name: debug-sleep
destructive_patterns.reason: DEBUG SLEEP blocks the Redis server and can cause availability issues.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
DEBUG SLEEP blocks the Redis server for the specified duration:

- All client connections freeze
- No commands are processed
- Cluster may mark node as failed
- Can trigger failover in Sentinel setups

This is meant for testing timeouts, not production use.

Disable in production:
  CONFIG SET debug-command no

destructive_patterns.name: shutdown
destructive_patterns.reason: SHUTDOWN stops the Redis server. Use carefully.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
SHUTDOWN stops the Redis server:

- SHUTDOWN SAVE: Saves RDB before exit (default)
- SHUTDOWN NOSAVE: Exits immediately, no save
- All client connections are closed
- Services depending on Redis fail

Check for unsaved changes:
  INFO persistence  # Look at rdb_changes_since_last_save

Ensure proper restart mechanism exists (systemd, Docker, etc.).

destructive_patterns.name: config-dangerous
destructive_patterns.reason: CONFIG SET for dir/dbfilename/slaveof can be used for security attacks.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
These CONFIG SET options are commonly exploited in Redis attacks:

- dir + dbfilename: Can write arbitrary files (RCE vector)
- slaveof/replicaof: Can exfiltrate data to attacker's server

Attack example:
1. CONFIG SET dir /var/spool/cron
2. CONFIG SET dbfilename root
3. SET payload '* * * * * malicious-command'
4. BGSAVE

Disable in production:
  rename-command CONFIG ""  # In redis.conf

Use ACLs to restrict these commands.

destructive_patterns.name: config-set-maxmemory
destructive_patterns.reason: CONFIG SET maxmemory can trigger immediate mass key eviction if new limit is below current usage.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
Lowering maxmemory below current usage causes Redis to evict keys immediately
according to the active eviction policy:

- volatile-lru/allkeys-lru: Silently deletes keys to fit budget
- noeviction: Returns OOM errors on writes

Check current usage first:
  INFO memory  # Look at used_memory vs maxmemory

Prefer gradual reduction or off-peak changes.

destructive_patterns.name: config-set-maxmemory-policy
destructive_patterns.reason: CONFIG SET maxmemory-policy changes how Redis evicts keys, risking silent data loss.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
Switching eviction policy can silently delete keys:

- noeviction -> allkeys-lru: Enables silent key deletion
- volatile-* -> allkeys-*: Extends eviction to non-expiring keys

Check current policy:
  CONFIG GET maxmemory-policy

Ensure application logic handles the new eviction behavior.

destructive_patterns.name: config-set-save
destructive_patterns.reason: CONFIG SET save can disable RDB persistence entirely, risking data loss on restart.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
CONFIG SET save "" disables all RDB snapshots:

- No automatic persistence to disk
- All data lost on restart unless AOF is enabled
- Existing RDB file may become stale

Check current persistence:
  CONFIG GET save
  CONFIG GET appendonly

Ensure at least one persistence mechanism remains active.

destructive_patterns.name: config-set-appendonly
destructive_patterns.reason: CONFIG SET appendonly can disable AOF persistence, risking data loss on restart.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
Disabling AOF without RDB means zero persistence:

- CONFIG SET appendonly no: Stops AOF logging
- Combined with disabled RDB: complete data loss on crash/restart

Check other persistence mechanisms:
  CONFIG GET save
  CONFIG GET appendonly

Ensure at least one persistence mechanism remains active.

destructive_patterns.name: config-rewrite
destructive_patterns.reason: CONFIG REWRITE persists all runtime CONFIG SET changes to redis.conf permanently.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
CONFIG REWRITE writes current runtime config back to redis.conf:

- Makes all CONFIG SET changes survive restarts
- Can persist dangerous temporary changes permanently
- No undo: must manually edit redis.conf to revert

Review current runtime changes first:
  CONFIG GET *  # Compare with original redis.conf

Ensure no dangerous CONFIG SET changes are pending before rewriting.

packs.name: SQLite
packs.description: Protects against destructive SQLite operations like DROP TABLE, DELETE without WHERE, and accidental data loss

destructive_patterns.name: drop-table
destructive_patterns.reason: DROP TABLE permanently deletes the table (even with IF EXISTS). Verify it is intended.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
DROP TABLE permanently removes a table and all its data from the SQLite database. Unlike some other databases, SQLite has no recycle bin or undo mechanism. The IF EXISTS clause only prevents errors, it doesn't make the operation less destructive.

Safer alternatives:
- .schema tablename: View table structure first
- .dump tablename: Export table data as SQL backup
- .backup: Create full database backup before dropping
- ALTER TABLE ... RENAME: Rename instead of drop if reorganizing

destructive_patterns.name: delete-without-where
destructive_patterns.reason: DELETE without WHERE deletes ALL rows. Add a WHERE clause.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
DELETE FROM without a WHERE clause removes every row from the table. This is almost always unintentional - if you truly want to remove all rows, TRUNCATE or DROP TABLE + CREATE is more explicit about the intent. SQLite doesn't support TRUNCATE, making this pattern especially dangerous.

Safer alternatives:
- SELECT COUNT(*) FROM table: Check row count first
- DELETE FROM table WHERE condition: Add explicit conditions
- .backup before DELETE: Create backup first
- Use transactions: BEGIN; DELETE ...; verify; COMMIT or ROLLBACK

destructive_patterns.name: vacuum-into
destructive_patterns.reason: VACUUM INTO overwrites the target file if it exists.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
VACUUM INTO creates a new compacted copy of the database at the specified path. If a file already exists at that path, it will be overwritten without warning. This can accidentally destroy other databases or important files.

Safer alternatives:
- Check if target file exists before running
- Use a unique filename with timestamp
- .backup filename: Alternative backup method
- Move existing file before VACUUM INTO

destructive_patterns.name: sqlite3-stdin
destructive_patterns.reason: Running SQL from file could contain destructive commands. Review the file first.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
Piping SQL from a file into sqlite3 executes all commands without review. The file may contain DROP TABLE, DELETE, or other destructive statements. If the file comes from an untrusted source or was auto-generated, it could cause unintended data loss.

Safer alternatives:
- Review the SQL file contents first
- .backup before running: Create database backup
- .read filename inside sqlite3: Allows Ctrl+C interruption
- Run in a transaction: Wrap file contents in BEGIN/COMMIT

packs.name: Supabase
packs.description: Protects against destructive Supabase CLI operations including database resets, migration rollbacks, function/secret/storage deletion, project removal, and infrastructure changes

destructive_patterns.name: supabase-db-reset
destructive_patterns.reason: supabase db reset drops and recreates the entire database. All data will be lost.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
supabase db reset completely destroys and recreates your database:

- All tables, views, and functions are dropped
- All data is permanently deleted
- Migrations are re-applied from scratch
- Seed data (if configured) is re-inserted

This is irreversible for any data not captured in migrations or seeds.
With --linked, this targets the remote production database.

Before resetting:
  supabase db dump -f backup.sql

Review differences first:
  supabase db diff

suggestions.description: Dump the database before resetting
Review schema differences before resetting
Check migration status before resetting

destructive_patterns.name: supabase-db-push
destructive_patterns.reason: supabase db push applies migrations to the remote database. Use --dry-run to preview first.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
supabase db push applies pending migrations to the remote (linked) database:

- Migrations may include DROP/ALTER statements
- Changes to the production database are irreversible
- With --linked, this targets the live project database

Always preview changes first:
  supabase db push --dry-run

Dump remote database before pushing:
  supabase db dump -f backup.sql --linked

suggestions.description: Preview migration changes without applying them
Review schema differences before pushing
Dump the remote database before pushing migrations

destructive_patterns.name: supabase-db-shell-destructive
destructive_patterns.reason: supabase db shell with destructive SQL (DROP/TRUNCATE/DELETE/ALTER). Verify the command carefully.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
supabase db shell is being invoked with destructive SQL:

- DROP permanently removes database objects
- TRUNCATE removes all rows from tables
- DELETE without WHERE removes all rows
- ALTER can drop columns, change types, or remove constraints

Dump the database before running destructive commands:
  supabase db dump -f backup.sql

Check row count first:
  supabase db shell -- -c 'SELECT COUNT(*) FROM tablename'

suggestions.description: Dump the database before running destructive SQL
Check row count before deleting

destructive_patterns.name: supabase-migration-repair
destructive_patterns.reason: supabase migration repair modifies the migration history. This can cause drift between schema and migrations.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
supabase migration repair alters the migration history table:

- Can mark migrations as applied or reverted
- May cause schema drift if used incorrectly
- Can break future migration runs

Review migration status first:
  supabase migration list

Dump the database before repairing:
  supabase db dump -f backup.sql

suggestions.description: Review current migration status before repairing
Dump the database before modifying migration history

destructive_patterns.name: supabase-migration-down
destructive_patterns.reason: supabase migration down reverts applied migrations. Schema changes and associated data may be lost.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
supabase migration down reverts previously applied migrations:

- Reversed migrations may DROP tables, columns, or constraints
- Data in dropped objects is permanently lost
- With --linked, this targets the remote production database
- The --version flag can revert multiple migrations at once

Review applied migrations first:
  supabase migration list

Dump the database before reverting:
  supabase db dump -f backup.sql --linked

suggestions.description: Review applied migrations before reverting
Dump the database before reverting migrations
Review schema differences before reverting

destructive_patterns.name: supabase-migration-squash
destructive_patterns.reason: supabase migration squash consolidates migrations and omits data manipulation statements (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE).
destructive_patterns.explanation:
supabase migration squash consolidates multiple migration files into one:

- DML statements (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE) are omitted
- Cron jobs, storage buckets, and vault secrets are lost
- These must be manually recreated in the squashed migration
- With --linked, modifies the remote migration history

Review migrations before squashing:
  supabase migration list

Dump the database before squashing:
  supabase db dump -f backup.sql

suggestions.description: Review migrations before squashing
Dump the database before squashing migrations

destructive_patterns.name: supabase-functions-delete
destructive_patterns.reason: supabase functions delete removes a deployed Edge Function. This causes immediate downtime for that function.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
supabase functions delete permanently removes a deployed Edge Function:

- The function is immediately unavailable
- Clients calling the function will receive errors
- The function can be re-deployed from local source code

Verify the function first:
  supabase functions list

Download source before deleting:
  supabase functions download {function_name}

suggestions.description: List functions to verify the correct one
Download function source before deleting

destructive_patterns.name: supabase-storage-rm
destructive_patterns.reason: supabase storage rm deletes objects from storage. With --recursive, entire directories are removed.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
supabase storage rm permanently deletes objects from Supabase Storage:

- Deleted files cannot be recovered
- With --recursive, entire directory trees are removed
- User-uploaded content may be permanently lost

List contents before deleting:
  supabase storage ls {path}

Copy files locally before deleting:
  supabase storage cp {path} ./backup/

suggestions.description: List storage contents before deleting
Copy files locally before deleting from storage

destructive_patterns.name: supabase-secrets-unset
destructive_patterns.reason: supabase secrets unset removes secrets from the project. Edge Functions depending on them will break immediately.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
supabase secrets unset removes environment variables from the project:

- Edge Functions depending on these secrets will fail
- Third-party integrations may break
- The secret values cannot be retrieved after removal

List secrets before removing:
  supabase secrets list

suggestions.description: List secrets to verify before removing

destructive_patterns.name: supabase-projects-delete
destructive_patterns.reason: supabase projects delete permanently removes the entire Supabase project and all its data.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
supabase projects delete permanently removes a Supabase project:

- The database and all data are deleted
- Auth users and sessions are removed
- Storage buckets and files are deleted
- Edge functions are removed
- API keys are invalidated

This action cannot be undone.

Dump the database before deleting:
  supabase db dump -f backup.sql --linked

Verify the project:
  supabase projects list

suggestions.description: Dump the database before deleting the project
List projects to verify the correct one

destructive_patterns.name: supabase-orgs-delete
destructive_patterns.reason: supabase orgs delete permanently removes the organization and may affect all projects within it.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
supabase orgs delete permanently removes a Supabase organization:

- All projects in the organization may be affected
- Billing and subscription are cancelled
- Team members lose access

This action cannot be undone.

List organization projects first:
  supabase projects list

Verify the organization:
  supabase orgs list

suggestions.description: List organizations to verify the correct one
List projects in the organization before deleting

destructive_patterns.name: supabase-branches-delete
destructive_patterns.reason: supabase branches delete permanently removes a preview branch and its database.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
supabase branches delete permanently removes a preview database branch:

- The branch database and all its data are deleted
- Any pending migrations on the branch are lost
- This action cannot be undone

Verify the branch first:
  supabase branches list

Inspect branch details:
  supabase branches get --id {branch_id}

suggestions.description: List branches to verify the correct one
Inspect branch details before deleting

destructive_patterns.name: supabase-domains-delete
destructive_patterns.reason: supabase domains delete removes the custom domain configuration. Clients using the custom domain will lose access.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
supabase domains delete removes the custom hostname configuration:

- Clients accessing the service via the custom domain will fail
- DNS records may need to be reconfigured
- Re-setup requires DNS verification

Check current domain configuration:
  supabase domains get

suggestions.description: Check current domain configuration before deleting

destructive_patterns.name: supabase-vanity-subdomains-delete
destructive_patterns.reason: supabase vanity-subdomains delete removes the vanity subdomain. Clients using it will lose access.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
supabase vanity-subdomains delete removes the vanity subdomain:

- Clients using the vanity URL will lose access
- Auth flows configured with the vanity URL will break
- The subdomain name may not be reclaimable

Check current configuration:
  supabase vanity-subdomains get

suggestions.description: Check current vanity subdomain before deleting

destructive_patterns.name: supabase-network-restrictions-update
destructive_patterns.reason: supabase network-restrictions update modifies allowed CIDR ranges. Misconfiguration can lock out all database connections.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
supabase network-restrictions update modifies the database firewall rules:

- Overly restrictive CIDR ranges can lock out all connections
- Including your own application servers and admin access
- Recovery may require Supabase support intervention

Check current restrictions first:
  supabase network-restrictions get

suggestions.description: Check current network restrictions before modifying

destructive_patterns.name: supabase-sso-remove
destructive_patterns.reason: supabase sso remove disconnects an SSO identity provider. All users authenticating via that provider will be locked out.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
supabase sso remove disconnects an SSO identity provider:

- All users authenticating via that provider are immediately locked out
- Existing sessions may be invalidated
- Re-adding the provider requires full reconfiguration

List providers before removing:
  supabase sso list

Inspect provider details:
  supabase sso show --id {provider_id}

suggestions.description: List SSO providers before removing
Inspect SSO provider details before removing

destructive_patterns.name: supabase-config-push
destructive_patterns.reason: supabase config push overwrites the remote project configuration with local config.toml settings.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
supabase config push replaces the remote project configuration:

- Auth, database, API, and other settings may be overwritten
- Misconfiguration can break authentication flows
- Changes take effect immediately on the live project

Review your local config.toml before pushing.

suggestions.description: Inspect the remote database state before pushing config

destructive_patterns.name: supabase-stop-no-backup
destructive_patterns.reason: supabase stop --no-backup stops the local stack and permanently deletes all data volumes.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
supabase stop --no-backup stops the local development stack and deletes data:

- All local database data is permanently deleted
- Storage objects in the local stack are removed
- Auth users created locally are lost
- Combined with --all, deletes ALL local Supabase projects' data

Use 'supabase stop' without --no-backup to preserve data.

suggestions.description: Stop local stack while preserving data backups

packs.name: Cassandra
packs.description: Protects against destructive Cassandra operations like DROP KEYSPACE, DROP TABLE, and TRUNCATE.

destructive_patterns.name: drop-keyspace
destructive_patterns.reason: DROP KEYSPACE permanently deletes the entire keyspace and all its data.

destructive_patterns.name: drop-table
destructive_patterns.reason: DROP TABLE permanently deletes the table and all its data.

destructive_patterns.name: truncate-table
destructive_patterns.reason: TRUNCATE permanently deletes all rows from a table.

packs.name: ClickHouse
packs.description: Protects against destructive ClickHouse operations like DROP DATABASE, DROP TABLE, and TRUNCATE.

destructive_patterns.name: drop-database
destructive_patterns.reason: DROP DATABASE permanently deletes the database and its data.

destructive_patterns.name: drop-table
destructive_patterns.reason: DROP TABLE permanently deletes the table data.

destructive_patterns.name: truncate-table
destructive_patterns.reason: TRUNCATE permanently deletes all data from a table.

destructive_patterns.name: alter-delete
destructive_patterns.reason: ALTER TABLE ... DELETE is a heavy mutation that deletes data.
destructive_patterns.explanation:
Mutations in ClickHouse are asynchronous and can be resource-intensive. A DELETE mutation without a careful WHERE clause can remove vast amounts of data.

Preview data to be deleted first with a SELECT.

packs.name: Snowflake
packs.description: Protects against destructive Snowflake operations like DROP DATABASE, DROP SCHEMA, and TRUNCATE.

destructive_patterns.name: drop-database
destructive_patterns.reason: DROP DATABASE permanently deletes the Snowflake database.

destructive_patterns.name: drop-schema
destructive_patterns.reason: DROP SCHEMA permanently deletes the schema and its objects.

destructive_patterns.name: drop-table
destructive_patterns.reason: DROP TABLE permanently deletes the table data.

destructive_patterns.name: truncate-table
destructive_patterns.reason: TRUNCATE permanently deletes all data from a table.
